identificationModule.briefTitle: PRIDE: Preventing Respiratory Illnesses During Childhood Study
identificationModule.officialTitle: A+ Head Start Intervention for Smoke Free Homes

collaborators.name: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI)

descriptionModule.briefSummary: Environmental tobacco smoke (ETS0, also known as secondhand smoke, is the combination of smoke given off by the burning end of a tobacco product and the smoke exhaled by the smoker. Children exposed to ETS are at an increased risk of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS), ear infections, colds, pneumonia, bronchitis and more severe asthma. ETS can also slow the growth of children's lungs and can cause them to cough, wheeze and fell breathless. The purpose of this study is to determine the effectiveness of a motivational interviewing-based program in reducing ETS exposure and improving lung health among children who are enrolled in a Head Start program and whose households include a smoker.
descriptionModule.detailedDescription:
About 90% of nonsmoking people in the US are exposed to ETS. More than 50 chemicals identified in ETS have been found to cause cancer and exposure has been linked to heart disease in adults and SIDS, ear infections and numerous respiratory problems, including asthma in children. In 2007, the Environmental Protection Agency and the Office of Head Start-a national program that provides economically disadvantaged children services to enhance their social and cognitive development-announced a new initiative to promote smoke-free homes for children in Head Start programs. Because Head Start reaches high-risk, low-income preschool children, it offers a timely intervention for reducing children's exposure to ETS. Head Start also attempts to engage parents, which is an important component of reducing household ETS exposure among children. This study will determine the effectiveness of a home-delivered, motivational interviewing-based program in reducing ETS exposure and improving lung health among children who are enrolled in the Baltimore City Head Start program and whose households include a smoker.

Participation in this study will last 1 year. First, all participating families will be visited at home by a study staff person who will attach special filters that will track the amount of nicotine in the various rooms of the house. During this initial visit, the participating children will undergo weight and height measurements and saliva sampling. About a week later, the filters will be collected, a 2nd saliva samples will be taken and parents will be interviewed about their family and child's health. Families will then be randomly assigned to one of two groups. Both groups will received educational information about reducing tobacco smoke exposure. One group will also receive the home-delivered, motivational interviewing-based program aimed to reduce ETS. This program will consist of 2 home visits and 2 phone calls, both led by health counselor who will teach participants how to reduce their child's exposure to tobacco smoke. The home visits will occur during Weeks 1 \& 2 and the phone calls will occur during Weeks 3 \& 6. Follow-up visits for all participating families will occur at Months 3,6 and 12 and will involve repeat filter testing, saliva monitoring and interviews.

maskingInfo.maskingDescription: For masking we were unable to mask the caregiver and child participant since they knew if they were receiving intervention component. The investigator was masked, but outcome assessor may have known group assignment.

armGroups.label: Behavioral
armGroups.description:
Motivational Interviewing Intervention Plus Education

Caregivers will receive a home-based motivational interviewing intervention for ETS reduction plus an educational program for ETS reduction.
interventionNames: Behavioral: Motivational Interviewing Intervention for ETS Reduction

armGroups.label: Education Only
armGroups.description: Caregivers will receive only educational program for ETS reduction.
interventionNames: Behavioral: Educational Program for ETS Reduction

interventions.name: Motivational Interviewing Intervention for ETS Reduction
interventions.description: The intervention is designed to motivate caregivers to reduce a child's ETS exposure by establishing a complete home and car smoking ban and by considering smoking cessation. Caregivers will receive 2 home visits \& 2 telephone session, both with a health counselor. Caregivers will be provided with feedback on air nicotine levels and child salivary cotinine levels. The main target for the intervention will be the primary caregiver of the child because the primary caregiver is ultimately responsible for protecting the child from ETS exposure. Any and all household members may participate in the intervention visits but are not required to do so.

interventions.name: Educational Program for ETS Reduction
interventions.description: An Environmental Protection Agency-based educational program that will consist of information about reducing tobacco smoke exposure.

primaryOutcomes.description: Air nicotine levels were an indicator of child's exposure to environmental tobacco smoke (ETS)

secondaryOutcomes.measure: ETS Reduction, as Measured by Child's Cotinine Levels
secondaryOutcomes.description: Child salivary cotinine will be a measure to evaluate environmental tobacco smoke (ETS) reduction

secondaryOutcomes.measure: Respiratory Function of Child by Self Report of Parent
secondaryOutcomes.description: Number of cold infections child experienced in previous 3 months, reported by caregiver

secondaryOutcomes.measure: Health Care Utilization by Child- Self Report From Parent/Caregiver
secondaryOutcomes.description: Parent caregiver reported urgent care visits, number of hospitalizations, and number of emergency department visits in the 12 months prior for child enrolled in study
secondaryOutcomes.timeFrame: Measured at baseline and 3, 6 and 12 months

secondaryOutcomes.measure: Number of Participants Who Report Endorsing a Home Smoking Ban
secondaryOutcomes.description: Number of participants endorsing presence of home smoking ban

eligibilityModule.eligibilityCriteria:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Child enrolled in Baltimore City Head Start
* Smoker living in the home with child

Exclusion Criteria:

* No smoker in home with child
* Does not speak English
* Is enrolled in other respiratory research study

references.citation: Eakin MN, Rand CS, Borrelli B, Bilderback A, Hovell M, Riekert KA. Effectiveness of motivational interviewing to reduce head start children's secondhand smoke exposure. a randomized clinical trial. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2014 Jun 15;189(12):1530-7. doi: 10.1164/rccm.201404-0618OC.

ipdSharingStatementModule.description: We will not be sharing individual participant data

participantFlowModule.preAssignmentDetails: Caregivers had to complete a baseline assessment prior to randomization and reported information for themselves and the targeted child due to the young age of the child. 350 caregivers were recruited but only 330 were randomized because 20 did not complete the baseline assessment.
participantFlowModule.recruitmentDetails: Caregivers of children were recruited from 16 Baltimore City Head Start programs from April 2009 to August 2012 with final data collection ending August 2013.

groups.title: Behavioral
groups.description:
Motivational Interviewing Intervention Plus Education

Participants will receive a home-based motivational interviewing intervention for ETS reduction plus an educational program for ETS reduction.

Motivational Interviewing Intervention for ETS Reduction: The intervention is designed to motivate families to reduce a child's ETS exposure by establishing a complete home and car smoking ban and by considering smoking cessation. Families will receive 2 home visits \& 2 telephone session, both with a health counselor. Families will be provided with feedback on air nicotine levels and child salivary cotinine levels. The main target for the intervention will be the primary caregiver of the child because the primary caregiver is ultimately responsible for protecting the child from ETS exposure. Any and all household members may participate in the intervention visits but are not required to do so.

groups.title: Education Only
groups.description:
Participants will receive only educational program for ETS reduction.

Educational Program for ETS Reduction: An Environmental Protection Agency-based educational program that will consist of information about reducing tobacco smoke exposure.

baselineCharacteristicsModule.populationDescription: Eligible caregivers had to be the parent or legal guardian of a child aged 6 months to 6 years, who reported one or more smokers living in the home, and who spoke English. The enrolled caregiver did not have to be the smoker.

outcomeMeasures.description: Child salivary cotinine will be a measure to evaluate environmental tobacco smoke (ETS) reduction
